plotlines.name: Hadji Murad's Escape Attempt and Death
plotlines.description: Hadji Murad, unable to rescue his family through Vorontsov's diplomacy and abandoned by his Avar allies, decides to flee the Russians at Nukha. He kills his Cossack escort during a ride on April 25, flees toward the mountains, becomes bogged down in flooded rice-fields, is surrounded by Karganov's militia and then Hadji Aga's 200 men, entrenches in a ditch, fights heroically while wounded, and is finally killed and beheaded.
plotlines.dramatic_function: Climax and tragic resolution of the protagonist's arc. The hero's last stand mirrors the Hamzad song he heard the night before, fulfilling the thistle frame.

plotlines.name: Rescue of Hadji Murad's Family
plotlines.description: Shamil holds Hadji Murad's wife, mother, and son Yusuf hostage in Vedeno, threatening to blind/kill the son. Hadji Murad offers 3000 rubles for their liberation. His Avar allies ultimately refuse out of fear of Shamil, forcing Hadji Murad's decision to flee and attempt rescue himself. The family's fate remains unresolved when he dies.
plotlines.dramatic_function: Primary motivation driving the hero's fatal decision. The unresolved hostage situation gives his death its tragic weight.

plotlines.name: Vorontsov Diplomacy and Nukha Relocation
plotlines.description: Hadji Murad implores Vorontsov to arrange a prisoner exchange for his family. Vorontsov stalls, awaiting Argutinski. Hadji Murad is granted relocation to Nukha. Kirillov arrives with stipend money; Hadji Murad humiliates him with a slap on the bald head, signaling breakdown of diplomatic trust.
plotlines.dramatic_function: Demonstrates the futility of Russian bureaucratic process and triggers Hadji Murad's decision to act independently.

plotlines.name: Butler's Gambling Downfall
plotlines.description: At Baryatinsky's farewell dinner for Kozlovsky, Butler breaks his vow not to gamble and loses 470 rubles. He writes to his brother and a stingy relative for loans, later borrows from a Jew at exorbitant interest. He sinks further into drink and makes a crude pass at Marya Dmitrievna, who rebuffs him.
plotlines.dramatic_function: Parallel moral decline mirrors larger themes of corruption; provides audience surrogate for witnessing Hadji Murad's death.

plotlines.name: Baryatinsky's New Command and Chechen Advance
plotlines.description: Prince Baryatinsky is appointed commander of the left flank, mustering a detachment in Kurin to continue the Tsar's campaign. Kozlovsky's farewell dinner marks the transition. Baryatinsky's affair with Marya Vasilevna creates tension; at end of April his detachment arrives at Butler's fort to advance through Chechnya.
plotlines.dramatic_function: Shows the Russian war machine grinding on indifferent to Hadji Murad; frames Russian aristocratic society's moral emptiness.

plotlines.name: Butler and Marya Dmitrievna Moral Tension
plotlines.description: After the gambling loss Butler courts Marya Dmitrievna grossly; she rebuffs him decisively. After Kamenev arrives with Hadji Murad's head, Marya Dmitrievna calls the soldiers 'cut-throats' and denounces war, becoming the moral voice of the chapter.
plotlines.dramatic_function: Marya Dmitrievna serves as moral witness condemning the barbarity of the victors; her outrage counterpoints the drunk officers' celebration.

plotlines.name: Major Petrov's Drunken Dissolution
plotlines.description: Major Petrov, very drunk and ash-pale, hacks at imaginary foes with his sword while singing of Shamil. He demands to kiss the severed head of Hadji Murad.
plotlines.dramatic_function: Embodies the degraded moral state of the Russian garrison; grotesque foil to Hadji Murad's dignity in death.

plotlines.name: Hadji Murad's Night of Decision
plotlines.description: Hadji Murad sits through the night weighing options: return to Shamil (who is 'a red liar' and will not trust him), remain with the Russians, or flee. The falcon-with-bells fable and memories of his son Yusuf, his young mother carrying him in a basket, and the Hamzad song crystallize his resolve to flee.
plotlines.dramatic_function: Interior climax; the tragic choice that seals his fate. Provides the hero's deepest psychological moment.

plotlines.name: Hamzad Song Prefigurement
plotlines.description: Khanefi sings the song of dzhigit Hamzad, who killed his horses as a bulwark and died fighting Russians, crying to birds to tell his mother. Hadji Murad hears it the night before his escape and later, in the rice-field shrubs, thinks 'we shall fight like Hamzad.'
plotlines.dramatic_function: Mythic foreshadowing; elevates Hadji Murad's death to epic register and frames it as conscious repetition of heroic pattern.

plotlines.name: Nightingale Song Motif
plotlines.description: Nightingales sing at dawn in Nukha when Hadji Murad plans his escape, then fall silent during the final firefight in the shrubs, then resume singing after his death.
plotlines.dramatic_function: Natural counterpoint to violence; indifference of nature to human tragedy echoes the thistle frame.

plotlines.name: The Cossack Escort (Nazarov and Comrades)
plotlines.description: Nazarov, young Old-Believer eldest son supporting his mother and siblings, leads the five-man Cossack escort. Brief biographical sketches establish each man before Hadji Murad's henchmen kill four of them; only Mishkin escapes to raise the alarm.
plotlines.dramatic_function: Tolstoyan authorial compassion — gives human weight to the 'enemy' victims; moral complication of Hadji Murad's heroism.

plotlines.name: The Severed Head Journey
plotlines.description: Hadji Aga beheads the corpse carefully to avoid soiling his shoes. Kamenev carries the head in a saddle-bag 'round to all the forts and aouls' as trophy-display. At Butler's fort he reveals it to Butler and Marya Dmitrievna; the drunk major kisses it.
plotlines.dramatic_function: Indictment of imperial barbarism; converts the hero's death into grotesque spectacle, triggering Marya Dmitrievna's denunciation.

plotlines.name: Thistle Frame Closure
plotlines.description: The narrator closes with the single line: 'It was of this death that I was reminded by the crushed thistle in the midst of the ploughed field.' Hadji Murad falls 'stretched out at full length like a thistle that had been mown down.'
plotlines.dramatic_function: Closes the novel's frame; establishes the thistle-as-resistance metaphor and universalizes Hadji Murad's death as image of trampled vitality.

plotlines.name: Falcon-with-Bells Fable
plotlines.description: Hadji Murad recalls a Tavlinian fable: a falcon captured by men and returning to his kind wearing silver jesses is pecked to death because he no longer belongs. He realizes Shamil's people would do the same to him.
plotlines.dramatic_function: Articulates the tragedy of the double exile — neither Russian nor free mountaineer any more.

plotlines.name: Shamil as Offstage Antagonist
plotlines.description: Shamil remains offstage but exerts decisive pressure: he holds the family, threatens to blind Yusuf, tortures anyone who helps. Hadji Murad imagines his 'pale, red-bearded face with half-closed eyes' in his dying vision.
plotlines.dramatic_function: Unseen pincer force that makes escape attempt suicidal; final antagonist image in Hadji Murad's dying mind.

plotlines.name: The Four Murids' Loyalty and Fate
plotlines.description: Hadji Murad's henchmen prepare weapons through the night. In the final battle Eldar is killed with a ram-like gaze at his master; Bata is wounded in the neck; Gamzalo sings with joy at each hit; Khan Mahoma sings prayers; Khanefi loads rifles. Three survive captured.
plotlines.dramatic_function: Ensemble tragic chorus; individualizes the tribal loyalty Hadji Murad commanded and shows different modes of warrior death/survival.

plotlines.name: Hadji Aga — Former Kunak Turned Killer
plotlines.description: Hadji Aga, once Hadji Murad's kunak who lived with him in the mountains, now leads 200 men against him alongside Akhmet Khan (son of Hadji Murad's old enemy). He personally strikes the killing dagger-blow and beheads the corpse.
plotlines.dramatic_function: Embodies the corruption of mountaineer bonds under Russian occupation; the betrayal makes the death intimate rather than impersonal.

plotlines.name: Karganov's Pursuit
plotlines.description: Karganov, military commander of the district, gives up the chase until an old man reveals he saw six horsemen in the rice-field shrubs. He surrounds the clump overnight and waits for dawn to take Hadji Murad alive or dead.
plotlines.dramatic_function: Introduces the encirclement mechanics; demonstrates role of native informant in imperial capture.

plotlines.name: 1000-Ruble Bounty
plotlines.description: After Mishkin's alarm the commander offers 1000 rubles for Hadji Murad alive or dead, and 200+ militia gallop out.
plotlines.dramatic_function: Mechanic of the manhunt; signals the imperial machinery's response speed and monetary logic.

plotlines.name: Kozlovsky's Farewell Speech
plotlines.description: At the dinner Kozlovsky, known for overusing the word 'how,' gives a stammering tearful speech of gratitude to the officers. Many eyes grow moist including Butler's.
plotlines.dramatic_function: Genuine soldierly emotion contrasted against the aristocratic schemes; sentiment immediately corrupted by the gambling that follows.

plotlines.name: Marya Vasilevna and Baryatinsky Affair
plotlines.description: Baryatinsky's open affair with Marya Vasilevna (Vorontsov's wife) causes officers and soldiers to be told off to lie in ambush at night to protect her from shelling, provoking their resentment.
plotlines.dramatic_function: Aristocratic decadence imposed on soldier bodies; moral corruption of the high command.

notes: CH 25 is climax: Hadji Murad's death plus the narrator's thistle frame closes the novel's outermost frame (opening thistle prologue → death). The Butler/garrison plotlines provide parallel moral commentary. Shamil never appears on-page in this range but remains the decisive offstage antagonist. Many micro-arcs (Cossack biographies, Kozlovsky speech) are classic Tolstoyan digressions cuttable for feature adaptation but essential to novel's moral architecture. A duplicate typo 'characters_invoved' existed in my draft of one entry and was corrected — final JSON uses 'characters_involved' consistently. Fusion candidates cluster around: (1) the main death-arc absorbing interior/motif/henchmen threads, (2) the Butler-garrison cluster absorbing Kozlovsky/affair/major-decline.
